fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

KARI J

INTRODUCTION

These reasons relate to orders sought by the applicant in the substantive proceedings, regarding an Application in a Proceeding filed on 20 March 2026.
The application has been determined on the papers, as requested by the applicant, and as r 5.13 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (“the Rules”), permits. In circumstances where the Court has refused the orders sought by the applicant, as discussed in these reasons, it was appropriate to deal with the same on an ex parte basis.

APPLICATION AND ORDERS SOUGHT

By his Application in a Proceeding filed 20 March 2026 the applicant seeks a raft of orders as follows:
That this Application be referred forthwith to a judge and determined urgently on the papers, in chambers without any attendance by the Applicant Father.
That the Applicant Father not be required to attend court or otherwise participate in person in relation to the hearing listed on 1 April 2026 unless and until this Application has first been determined.
That no party be permitted to press for a hearing on 1 April 2026 or for any harmful proceedings Order against the Applicant unless and until this Application has been determined.
That the hearing listed on l April 2026 be vacated and adjourned and relisted on a date not earlier than 28 days thereafter, or on such other date as the Court considers fit
That the Applicant Father have leave to rely on his reply affidavit and any responsive material out of time.
That the question whether a harmful proceedings order pursuant to section 102QAC should be made against the Applicant Father be stayed, deferred, or stood over until: a. the Applicant Father's Application in a Proceeding filed 10 February 2026 concerning protected confidences / sensitive material is determined;
b. the Applicant Father's responsive material is filed and relied upon; and
c. the Independent Children's Lawyer has stated their position on the Applicant Father's pending applications.
That Justice Kari recuse herself/ be disqualified from further hearing or determination of the interlocutory applications presently listed for 1 April 2026.
Alternatively, if recusal is not granted, that the issue of recusal / disqualification be formally preserved and recorded, with liberty to the Applicant Father to seek review I appeal if so advised.
That the Application in a Proceeding filed by the Applicant Father on 12 March 2026 be treated as void, withdrawn, or superseded by the Application in a Proceeding filed on 13 March 2026, and not be heard or counted as a separate live application.
That the Independent Children's Lawyer file and serve, by a short date, a substantive affidavit or written position stating: a. whether the ICL supports or opposes the Applicant Father's Applications in a Proceeding filed 22 December 2025, 10 February 2026, 12 March 2026, and 13 March 2026;
b. whether the ICL has met the child in accordance with section 68LA(5A); and
c. if not, the basis on which the ICL says the requirement does not presently apply.
That, pending further order, the Respondent Mother and the Independent Children's Lawyer not rely upon, publish, or further use: a. any psychiatric, hospital, counselling, medication, or other sensitive medical material the subject of the Applicant Father's 10 February 2026 application; and
b. any Department for Child Protection/ SAPOL material said to arise from the April 2024 incident, unless and until the Court determines the Applicant Father's objections and/or grants leave.
Alternatively, that the materials in Order 8 be treated as objected to and not relied upon for the 1 April 2026 hearing unless and until the objections are determined.
That the Respondent Mother and/or the Independent Children's Lawyer produce: a. the original handwritten and signed heads of agreement made at Family Dispute Resolution in late October/ early November 2025;
b. any later typed or modified version of those heads of agreement presented to the Applicant Father in court for signature; and
c. any file notes, covering correspondence, or drafts evidencing the change between the FDR version and the later court version.
That the Applicant Father be granted access to and liberty to obtain: a. the transcript of the relevant hearings and mentions, including any hearing at which the interim consent terms were read out; and
b. if transcript is unavailable, the underlying audio recording or such access to the recording as the Court directs.
That the Registry /transcript provider provide a written update as to the status of the transcript order already placed by the Applicant Father and the reason the transcript has not been produced.
That, if the hearing is not adjourned generally, it be limited to the preliminary procedural issues raised by this Application, including adjournment, leave, recusal, protected confidences/ sensitive material, transcript access, the status of the 12 and 13 March applications, and the JCL position, and that the substantive parenting issues and any harmful proceedings issue be stood over.
That costs be reserved, or alternatively that costs of this application be costs in the cause.
Such further or other case-management orders as are necessary to restore procedural fairness.

BACKGROUND

To understand the application that has now been made by the applicant something needs to be understood about the substantive proceedings and the procedural history of the litigation.
These are parenting proceedings concerning the parties five year old child X born in 2021.
The proceedings were commenced by the father in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2), when he filed an Initiating Application on 15 December 2022 in which he sought amongst other things an urgent recovery order.
Whilst the mother joined financial issues to the proceedings on 5 November 2024 (with the filing of an Amended Response to Initiating Application), the financial proceedings resolved with orders made by consent on 5 August 2025.
The father is currently 50 years of age and the mother is currently 43 years of age.
The parties were living together in a relationship from between October 2019 (on the mother’s case) or February 2020 (on the father’s case), until approximately November 2022; living separately and apart under the same roof until 5 December 2022. The parties met through a dating app, with the father travelling to meet the mother in her home of City B, Country C and the parties’ travelling to Australia to continue their relationship in January 2020. The mother now has permanent residency in Australia.
On the mother’s case at the time of separation, she moved into emergency housing with the child, provided by a domestic violence service. She says the child remained in her care until the father retained him after a period of time spending on 16 December 2022.
When the proceedings first came before the Court on 21 December 2022 an order was made by a judge for the child to be delivered up to the mother and thereafter the child’s care was to be rotated between the parties on a daily basis. In addition, an order was made appointing an Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) to promote the interests of the child.
It is the mother’s case that the relationship was marred by family violence which included coercive and controlling behaviour. It is also the mother’s case that she has always been the child’s primary carer.
The father disputes these assertions and makes allegations that the mother has mental health issues. It is apparent from the court file however that concerns have existed in relation to the mental health of both parents, with orders made on 28 April 2023 for the father to provide a report from his treating psychiatrist and the mother to undertake an independent psychiatric assessment.
On 1 February 2023 an order was made by a judicial registrar pursuant to s 91B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) inviting the Department for Child Protection to intervene in the proceedings. That order has led to the preparation of a number of reports by the Department, with the initial view taken that they ought monitor and observe the child’s circumstances, but ultimately declining the Court’s request to intervene in May 2023.
On 6 February 2023 an order was made by a judge varying the child’s living arrangements such that they were to be rotated between the parties each second day rather than each day.
At a hearing on 22 March 2023 a judge made an order which provided for the child to live with the mother and spend time with the father between 9.00am and 5.00pm each Monday, Wednesday and Saturday. Those orders were varied by orders made 21 August 2023 by consent between the parties such that the father’s time commenced at 6.45am and concluded at 3.15pm.
By orders made by a senior judicial registrar on 17 December 2024 the father’s time spending on each Monday, Wednesday and Saturday was further varied such that it take place between 7.30am and 5.00pm.
At a compliance and readiness hearing on 8 August 2025, the proceedings were transferred to this Court. Orders were also made for the parties to participate in a dispute resolution conference.
Following the transfer of the proceedings to this Court, orders were made in chambers by me on 15 September 2025 listing the proceedings for a case management hearing on 1 October 2025 as the registry’s case management judge.
At the hearing on 1 October 2025, the Court was amongst other things advised:
(a) The dispute resolution conference had yet to take place;

(b) The father intended to file an urgent application for the change of the child’s residence; and

(c) The father raised concerns about the mother’s partner.

Orders were ultimately made that day listing the proceedings for further case management on 11 November 2025, with orders made that any application filed by the father be made specially returnable to that date for “mention only”.
Shortly thereafter on 3 October 2025 (sealed 7 October), the father filed his foreshadowed Application in a Proceeding, together with a number of other documents. Orders were made in chambers on 9 October 2025 for the application to be listed for mention only to the existing hearing on 11 November 2025 as had been contemplated and ordered at the hearing on 1 October 2025.
The parties attended a dispute resolution conference on 27 October 2025 and agreed at that event to continue their negotiations, with a further conference taking place on 4 November 2025.
At the hearing before me on 11 November 2025 the Court was advised by the parties that interim agreement had been reached and the Court was invited and made orders by consent, providing for the father’s time spending with the child over a fortnightly cycle prior to and following the child starting school in July 2026. At this juncture, in that cycle, the father’s time spending was to occur in week one from the 9.00am to 5.00pm on Monday and from 9.00am Saturday until 5.00pm Sunday, and in week two from 9.00am Wednesday until commencement of pre-school on Thursday and from 9.00am to 5.00pm on Saturday. These orders have remained in place since that time.
The making of those orders resulted in and included the dismissal of the father’s Application in a Proceeding filed 3 October 2025 by consent.
Orders were separately made on 11 November 2025 referring the proceedings to the pool of matters awaiting trial and for the preparation of an updated Family Report; there having previously been a Child Impact Report prepared and dated 27 April 2023 and a Family Report prepared and dated 5 September 2024.
Thereafter, a further Application in a Proceeding was filed by the father on 22 December 2025. The application was returnable to a hearing before a judicial registrar on 28 January 2026 with banal procedural orders made that day to ready the matter for a hearing on 1 April 2026 before a senior judicial registrar.
By his Application in a Proceeding filed 22 December 2025, the father asks the Court to make orders which include an order that the child live with him and the mother to spend time with the child on alternate weekends from 5.00pm Friday until 5.00pm Sunday. It is apparent from the affidavit filed in support of that application that the father is concerned by the mother’s residential move from City D to City E, and the impact of the same on the time spending arrangements which had been ordered on 11 November 2025. It is unnecessary at this stage to say anything further about that application as it will be determined on its merit once argument has been heard.
Since the filing and listing (to 1 April 2026) of that interlocutory application the father has filed:
(a) An Application for Review on 2 February 2026;

(b) An Application in a Proceeding on 10 February 2026;

(c) An Application in a Proceeding on 12 March 2026;

(d) An Application in a Proceeding on 13 March 2026; and

(e) The within Application in a Proceeding on 20 March 2026.

The Review Application filed 2 February 2026 was dismissed by orders made by Justice McNabb on 12 March 2026.
The Applications in a Proceeding filed 10 February 2026 and 12 March 2026 were all listed to the existing hearing before the Senior Judicial Registrar on 1 April 2026.
Upon the filing of the Application in a Proceeding on 13 March 2026 the proceedings were referred to my chambers as the case management judge.
An order was made by me in chambers on 16 March 2026 listing all of the extant applications before me instead of the senior judicial registrar, and preserving the hearing date of 1 April 2026. Amongst the factors taken into account in making that procedural order, regard was had to the following:
(a) The number of applications that had been filed since the hearing on 11 November 2025; and

(b) Concern that the senior judicial registrar would not have sufficient time to hear all of the applications at the hearing on 1 April 2026.

In the father’s affidavit filed in support of the Application in a Proceeding filed 13 March 2026 he asserted that at the review hearing before McNabb J, his Honour had raised that he was considering making a harmful proceedings order directed to the father pursuant to s 102QAC of the Act (paragraphs 29-31 of the father’s affidavit filed 13 March 2026).
Whilst the Court does not have the benefit of transcript from that hearing, it is perhaps unsurprising that his Honour made some comment about the possibility of a harmful proceedings order being made in circumstances where the mother has sought that order in her Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed 27 February 2026 (at paragraph 2), which has already been listed for hearing together with the father’s various applications to be heard on 1 April 2026.
Again, no more needs to be said about this topic because the issue will be heard on its merits as part of the proceedings to be determined by the Court at the hearing on 1 April 2026. With this in mind and to ensure that the applicant understood that this issue was to be traversed by the Court at the hearing on 1 April 2026, as part of the orders made in chambers by me on 16 March 2026, an order was made directing the parties to be in a position to make submissions at the hearing on 1 April 2026 as to whether a harmful proceedings order (pursuant to Part XIB, Division 1B of the Act), ought be made directed to the father. As the father is self represented those orders directed the father to the relevant provisions in the Act so that he was in position to consider the same in advance of the hearing.

THE CURRENT APPLICATION

It is apparent that the tenor of the present interlocutory application is both an adjournment of the hearing on 1 April 2026, but additionally a raft of procedural orders directed to that hearing and the proceedings more generally.
At 10.21am this day the father sent by email written submissions to the Court under cover of the following:
As an urgent measure, given the circumstances, I ask that these two attached written submissions be referred/flagged to the registry or chambers. My Application in a proceeding filed 20 March (urgent) is currently in Chambers. The Application and submissions are self-explanatory. This is now over 2 days before the hearing.
Despite those documents not having been formally filed with the court, regard has been had to the same to the extent that they relate to the present application only at this juncture.

The Disqualification Application

At paragraph 9 of the present Application in a Proceeding the father asks the Court to make an order that I be recused from hearing the pending interlocutory proceedings. It is therefore necessary and appropriate that this aspect of the application is considered first.
In the affidavit filed in support of the application, under the heading “Recusal due to Apprehended Bias” the father sets out the basis upon which he asks that I be disqualified from hearing the interlocutory applications. In essence it appears that the father considers that he will not “receive a fair hearing” if I were to hear the applications (paragraph 105 of the father’s affidavit filed 20 March 2026). He says that this is so, pleading in a generalised sense that at the (presumably two) case management hearings before me he was “repeatedly cut off, spoken over, and not given a fair opportunity to explain [his] position” (paragraph 106 of the father’s affidavit filed 20 March 2026). Complaint was also made by the father of being interrupted by the opposing legal representatives, including counsel for the ICL. The father additionally is concerned that the interlocutory hearings have been moved from a hearing before a senior judicial registrar to a hearing before a judge and that the orders made on 16 March 2026 make directions about the harmful proceedings order issue.
The legal principles to be applied whenever there is a question over the independence or impartiality of a judge, are well settled (Charisteas v Charisteas [2021] HCA 29; (2021) 273 CLR 289 at [11]) and were identified by the High Court in Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy [2000] HCA 63; (2000) 205 CLR 337 (“Ebner”) in the following terms:
Where, in the absence of any suggestion of actual bias, a question arises as to the independence or impartiality of a judge (or other judicial officer or juror), as here, the governing principle is that, subject to qualifications relating to waiver (which is not presently relevant) or necessity (which may be relevant to the second appeal), a judge is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question the judge is required to decide. That principle gives effect to the requirement that justice should both be done and be seen to be done, a requirement which reflects the fundamental importance of the principle that the tribunal be independent and impartial. It is convenient to refer to it as the apprehension of bias principle.
The apprehension of bias principle may be thought to find its justification in the importance of the basic principle, that the tribunal be independent and impartial. So important is the principle that even the appearance of departure from it is prohibited lest the integrity of the judicial system be undermined. There are, however, some other aspects of the apprehension of bias principle which should be recognised. Deciding whether a judicial officer (or juror) might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of a question that has not been determined requires no prediction about how the judge or juror will in fact approach the matter. The question is one of possibility (real and not remote), not probability. Similarly, if the matter has already been decided, the test is one which requires no conclusion about what factors actually influenced the outcome. No attempt need be made to inquire into the actual thought processes of the judge or juror.
The apprehension of bias principle admits of the possibility of human frailty. Its application is as diverse as human frailty. Its application requires two steps. First, it requires the identification of what it is said might lead a judge (or juror) to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits. The second step is no less important. There must be an articulation of the logical connection between the matter and the feared deviation from the course of deciding the case on its merits. The bare assertion that a judge (or juror) has an “interest” in litigation, or an interest in a party to it, will be of no assistance until the nature of the interest, and the asserted connection with the possibility of departure from impartial decision making, is articulated. Only then can the reasonableness of the asserted apprehension of bias be assessed.
(Footnotes omitted)

(Bold emphasis added)
In answer to the two stage test posed in Ebner:
(a) As best understood from the affidavit filed by the father, his complaint relates to conduct on my part.

(b) There is however no articulation by the father in relation to the second stage.

The father’s inability to address the second stage of the test in Ebner is fatal to the disqualification application that he has now made.
More importantly, understanding that aside from the consent orders made on 11 November 2025 at the request of the parties, case management decisions and procedural orders have only been made by me at this juncture, and that I have not been required to exercise discretion and determine any contested applications in these proceedings to date, it is difficult to understand the complaints now made by the father as ones which could ground any apprehended bias assertion.
To that end, the father, and indeed both parties ought understand that Part VII, Division 12A of the Act), empowers and indeed requires the Court, when conducting child related proceedings to take up active case management, so as to limit the impact of proceedings on the children who are the subject of those proceedings. The principles set out at ss 102NE, 102NF and 102NG of the Act provide:
102NE Principles for conducting child-related proceedings and property or other proceedings

Application of the principles
(1) The court must give effect to the principles in this section:
(a) in performing duties and exercising powers (whether under this Division or otherwise) in relation to child‑related proceedings or property or other non-child-related proceedings; and

(b) in making other decisions about the conduct of child‑related proceedings or property or other non-child-related proceedings.

Failure to do so does not invalidate the proceedings or any order made in them.
(2) Regard is to be had to the principles that are relevant to the particular proceedings in interpreting this Division.
Note: All the principles are relevant to child-related proceedings. The principles in subsection (3), paragraph (5)(a) and subsection (6) do not apply in relation to property or other non-child-related proceedings.

Principle 1
(3) The first principle is that the court is to consider the needs of the child concerned and the impact that the conduct of the proceedings may have on the child in determining the conduct of the proceedings.
Principle 2
(4) The second principle is that the court is to actively direct, control and manage the conduct of the proceedings.
Principle 3
(5) The third principle is that the proceedings are to be conducted in a way that will safeguard:
(a) the child concerned from being subjected to, or exposed to, abuse, neglect or family violence; and

(b) the parties to the proceedings against family violence.

Principle 4
(6) The fourth principle is that the proceedings are, as far as possible, to be conducted in a way that will promote cooperative and child‑focused parenting by the parties.
Principle 5
(7) The fifth principle is that the proceedings are to be conducted without undue delay and with as little formality, and legal technicality and form, as possible.
102NF This Division also applies to proceedings in Chambers

The following persons, when hearing child‑related proceedings or property or other non‑child‑related proceedings in Chambers, have all of the duties and powers that a court has under this Division:
(a) in the case of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1)—a Judge, the Chief Executive Officer, or a Senior Registrar or Registrar of the Court;
(b) in the case of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2)—a Judge, the Chief Executive Officer, or a Senior Registrar or Registrar of the Court;

(c) in any other case—a Judge, Registrar or magistrate.
Note: An order made in Chambers has the same effect as an order made in open court

102NG Powers under this Division may be exercised on court’s own initiative

The court may exercise a power under this Division:
(a) on the court’s own initiative; or

(b) at the request of one or more of the parties to the proceedings.
In order to give effect to those principles, the Court is invested with a range of duties and powers as follows:
102NH General duties
(1) In giving effect to the principles in section 102NE, the court must:
(a) ask each party to the proceedings whether the party considers that the party, or another party to the proceedings, has been, or is at risk of being, subjected to family violence; and

(b) in child‑related proceedings—ask each party to the proceedings whether the party considers that the child concerned has been, or is at risk of being, subjected to, or exposed to, abuse, neglect or family violence; and

(c) decide which of the issues in the proceedings require full investigation and hearing and which may be disposed of summarily; and

(d) decide the order in which the issues are to be decided; and

(e) give directions or make orders about the timing of steps that are to be taken in the proceedings; and

(f) in deciding whether a particular step is to be taken—consider whether the likely benefits of taking the step justify the costs of taking it; and

(g) make appropriate use of technology; and

(h) if the court considers it appropriate—encourage the parties to use family dispute resolution or, in child‑related proceedings, family counselling; and

(i) deal with as many aspects of the matter as it can on a single occasion; and

(j) deal with the matter, where appropriate, without requiring the parties’ physical attendance at court.
(2) Subsection (1) does not limit subsection 102NE(1).

(3) A failure to comply with subsection (1) does not invalidate an order.

102NJ Power to make determinations, findings and orders at any stage of proceedings
(1) If, at any time after the commencement of child‑related proceedings or property or other non‑child‑related proceedings and before making final orders, the court considers that it may assist in the determination of the dispute between the parties, the court may do any or all of the following:
(a) make a finding of fact in relation to the proceedings;

(b) determine a matter arising out of the proceedings;

(c) make an order in relation to an issue arising out of the proceedings.
Note: For example, the court may choose to use this power if the court considers that making a finding of fact at a particular point in the proceedings will help to focus the proceedings.
(2) Subsection (1) does not prevent the court doing something mentioned in paragraph (1)(a), (b) or (c) at the same time as making final orders.

(3) To avoid doubt, a person who exercises a power under subsection (1) in relation to proceedings is not, merely because of having exercised the power, required to disqualify himself or herself from a further hearing of the proceedings.

Importantly, any of the duties and powers set out in s 102NH and s 102NJ of the Act can be exercised by the Court of either its own volition, or at the request of one or more of the parties to the proceedings (s 102NG of the Act).
Those principles and powers ought also be considered together with the overarching purpose as contained in s 95 and s 96 of the Act, which provide as follows:
95 Overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions
(1) The overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions is to facilitate the just resolution of disputes:
(a) in a way that ensures the safety of families and children; and

(b) in relation to proceedings under this Act in which the best interests of a child are the paramount consideration—in a way that promotes the best interests of the child; and

(c) according to law; and

(d) as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.
Note: For family law practice and procedure provisions, see subsection (4).
(2) Without limiting subsection (1), the overarching purpose includes the following objectives in relation to proceedings under this Act:
(a) the just determination of all such proceedings;

(b) the efficient use of the judicial and administrative resources available for the purposes of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(c) the efficient disposal of the overall caseload of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(d) the disposal of all such proceedings in a timely manner;

(e) the resolution of disputes at a cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.
(3) The family law practice and procedure provisions must be interpreted and applied, and any power conferred or duty imposed by them (including the power to make applicable Rules of Court) must be exercised or carried out, in the way that best promotes the overarching purpose.

(4) The family law practice and procedure provisions are the following, so far as they apply in relation to proceedings under this Act:

(a) the applicable Rules of Court;

(b) any other provision made by or under this Act, or any other Act, with respect to the practice and procedure of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia or any other court.
96 Duty to act consistently with the overarching purpose

Duty of parties
(1) The parties to proceedings under this Act must conduct the proceedings (including negotiations for settlement of the dispute to which the proceedings relate) in a way that is consistent with the overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions.
Duty of lawyers
(2) A party’s lawyer must, in the conduct of proceedings under this Act on the party’s behalf (including in the conduct of negotiations for settlement of the dispute to which the proceedings relate):
(a) take account of the duty imposed on the party by subsection (1); and

(b) assist the party to comply with the duty.
Estimate of costs
(3) In proceedings under this Act a court may, for the purpose of enabling a party to comply with the duty imposed by subsection (1), require the party’s lawyer to give the party an estimate of:
(a) the likely duration of the proceedings or part of the proceedings; and

(b) the likely amount of costs that the party will have to pay in connection with the proceedings or part of the proceedings (including the costs that the lawyer will charge to the party).
Costs orders
(4) In exercising the discretion to award costs in proceedings under this Act, a court must take account of any failure to comply with the duty imposed by subsection (1) or (2).

(5) Without limiting the exercise of that discretion, a court may order a party’s lawyer to bear costs personally.

(6) If a court orders a lawyer to bear costs personally because of a failure to comply with the duty imposed by subsection (2), the lawyer must not recover the costs from the lawyer’s client.

These powers of the Court might give the impression at times that a judicial officer is overtly directive of proceedings. It is however a necessary element of ensuring that the best interests of children are at all times the paramount concern of the Court and that the proceedings can be progressed through the Court ensuring the safety of families and children and as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.
In his written submissions emailed to the Court on 30 March 2026:
(a) The father makes complaint that he was not afforded the opportunity to be heard about the hearing on 1 April 2026 being shifted from a senior judicial registrar to a judge. The father was not required to be heard about the same, and no valid complaint can be made in that regard.

(b) The father asserts that I have delivered a series of judgment in what the father described as “concurrent management” of proceedings reported as Vaughan and Vaughan. While it is correct that I have delivered a number of judgments in those separate proceedings, the father asserts that because he considers there to be similarities between those proceedings and these proceedings a “fair-minded lay observer aware of that parallel would reasonably apprehend that the approach to the present matter may have been shaped by that intensive experience” (page 5 of the father’s written submissions filed 26 March 2026). This allegation is obnoxious and entirely without merit, and if given any credence would have the effect of every judicial officer in any Court facing apprehended bias applications because they have discharged their judicial function of hearing and determining cases that come before them on their respective merits.

The father asserts that his apprehended bias application “must” be determined by a judge other than myself, asserting the same to be “an absolute principle”. The father however is wrong in that regard (page 5 of the father’s written submissions filed 26 March 2026).For all of these reasons, the father’s application that I be disqualified from hearing the interlocutory proceedings fails. Paragraph 10 of the father’s application in the circumstances falls away.

The Adjournment Application

By paragraphs 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8 and 24 of the Application in a Proceeding filed 20 March 2026 the father ostensibly asks the Court to adjourn various applications and interlocutory issues.
The father’s application for an adjournment appears to be principally grounded in his alleged poor health.
Annexed to the father’s affidavit filed in support of the application, the father has attached two separate medical certificates both dated 19 March 2026 and both authored by Dr F.
The first of those two certificates sets out (on page 15 of the affidavit):
I [Dr F] hereby certify that I have examined the above-named patient. The patient is suffering from a medical condition and is unfit for his usual activities.

[Mr Tordai] may not attend their normal activities from 19/03/2026 to 26/03/2026 (inclusive) due to illness.
The second of those medical certificates sets out (on page 16 of the affidavit):
I have seen [Mr Tordai] today. He has been a patient of our practice since 2021. I understand he is going through family court and due to present at an Interim Defended Hearing on the 1st of April 2026. He is experiencing significant stress and duress as he is self represented due to financial pressures.

In addition to the stress of the upcoming hearing, he is currently unwell with a URTI and is unable to adequately prepare for this hearing. We ask that he be provided with more time for his recovery and then better prepare himself for the eventual hearing.
Whilst those medical certificates set out somewhat contradictory information, but taking them at face value, the first observation that can be made is that the first of those certificates identifies that the father was unwell between 19 and 26 March 2026. On any view therefore he will be well enough for the hearing on 1 April 2026, and have a number of days prior to the same to prepare.
In Saidov & Saidov (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC2F 223 Judge Parker (as she then was) set out the following helpful summary regarding adjournment applications grounded in the provision of a medical certificate in the following terms:
The relevant principles as to what constitutes an adequate medical certificate for the purposes of adjourning proceedings or explaining non-attendance are set out in UTSG Pty Ltd v Sydney Metro (No 5) as follows:
While each case turns on its facts, the medical evidence should, at a minimum, answer the central question of why – and not just whether – the medical condition will prevent a litigant from participating in a court hearing either in person or by some other means (for example, by telephone). It is this nexus that is critical.
To be sufficient, the medical evidence should identify in broad terms the medical condition that the person is suffering from, the symptoms of that condition insofar as they are relevant to a litigant’s participation in a court hearing, the severity of the condition, and its expected duration. The doctor providing the certificate must be clearly identified and the certificate must be signed and dated.
Absent this bare minimum the opposing party cannot, as a matter of fairness, test the cogency of the medical evidence and the Court does not have sufficient information before it to meaningfully exercise its discretion to grant the vacation sought. To the extent that the provision of an appropriately detailed medical certificate to the Court (and to the opposing party) results in a loss of privacy, this is the price that must be paid by a litigant in order for the Court to grant the indulgence of a vacation of hearing dates, a course that a Court does not undertake lightly having regard to the inevitable increased cost to the parties of the delay and the inefficient allocation of Court resources that results.
Justice Barrett in the Supreme Court of New South Wales similarly held as follows in Magjarraj v Asteron Life Limited
All too frequently judges see cryptic written statements from medical practitioners referring to some undisclosed and undiagnosed “medical condition”, culminating in the assertion that a person is unfit to attend court or unfit for a court hearing. To the extent that such statements are put forward as evidence about the state of the person’s health and the extent to which impaired health may incapacitate a person from participating in court proceedings, the statements fail the most fundamental test for the reception of expert evidence. Bald unexplained and unfathomable statements of that kind must be simply rejected out of hand as evidence of anything.
(Footnotes omitted)
I am satisfied that the first of the father’s medical certificates falls significantly short of providing sufficient evidence to enable the Court to understand the father’s health circumstances so as to warrant the adjournment of the upcoming hearing.
In relation to the second of the two medical certificates, that document appears to provide contradictory information to the first. In addition, the bare assertion that the father is suffering from “stress” and “duress” without more, is entirely unhelpful and meaningless. Most litigants facing litigation in this Court are likely to be stressed, sadly it is the nature of litigation. It is not however a basis for an adjournment of proceedings, rather it speaks to progressing the proceedings without further delay so as to minimise the stressful effect of litigation upon all of the parties to the proceedings.
It must otherwise be remembered that the father first made an interlocutory application on 22 December 2025. The father makes complaint that the case management systems of the Court have been unsatisfactory and that there has been delays in hearing the first of these applications. It is therefore difficult to reconcile the father’s current request that the proceedings be adjourned.
For these reasons, and taking into account the Court’s case management powers and obligations, the father’s adjournment application is refused. Court time has been allocated to the proceedings and there does not appear to be a proper basis to squander that time.

Procedural and Case Management Orders Sought

By paragraph 5 of the present application the father asks the Court to allow him to rely on responsive material filed out of time. Fatal to this application is that the father does not articulate nor identify what that material is. That application shall therefore be dismissed.
By paragraph 11 of the present application the father asks the Court to dismiss his Application in a Proceeding filed 12 March 2026. That order shall therefore be made.
By paragraphs 12 to 14 of his application the father seeks to direct the filing of the material by the ICL. This is not an order the father can direct the Court to make and it shall be dismissed.
By paragraphs 15 to 17, the father seeks to limit the use of material by the mother and the ICL. This order however is unnecessary given firstly the implied undertaking that exists to limit the use to which documents obtained by compulsion in these proceedings can be put in proceedings other than these, but secondly in light of the provisions in Part XIVB of the Act.
If the father is seeking to limit the use of certain documents (psychiatric records and reports in relation to him) in the context of the within litigation generally, without more that would be too broad an order to make. If there is an objection at any later stage of the proceedings to the use of specific documents obtained in the proceedings, that objection can be made at that juncture and will be heard and determined on its merits. Accordingly, this application fails.
By paragraphs 18 to 20 the father asks the mother and the ICL to produce the handwritten heads of agreement signed at dispute resolution in October/November 2025, the typed version thereafter prepared and communications exchanged. Whilst these are documents that the father ought already have in his possession, out of an abundance of caution the Court shall make an order in relation to the original copy of the signed heads of agreement, together with the copy thereafter signed and lodged with the Court.
By paragraphs 21 to 23 the father seeks orders pertaining to the provision of transcripts and the time frame for providing the same. These orders however are not ones the Court ought make. Administrative arrangements exist for parties to obtain transcripts. The father ought utilise those arrangements. In relation to the orders sought by the father regarding access to the audio recording, there does not appear to be any proper basis at this stage for that application to have been made. This application shall be dismissed.
For all of these reasons, and save and except as to the documents to be provided by the ICL, the father’s Application in a Proceeding of 20 March 2026 shall be dismissed.
